risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomisation is undertaken by central allocation using a computerized random number generator at the Internet Data and Information Center for Medical Research (INDICE), provided by the University Hospital Medical Information Network (UMIN) in Japan... randomisation is performed to assign patients, in a 1:1 allocation ratio... with stratification by sex, age ... and assumed duration of illness ... via the minimisation method." Comment: The sequence was generated using a computerized random number generator, which is a truly random method. Although minimisation/stratification is mentioned, the paper explicitly states the random number generator used for allocation, so this domain is low risk.

Quote: "To avoid biased allocation, randomisation is undertaken by central allocation using a computerized random number generator at the Internet Data and Information Center for Medical Research (INDICE), provided by the University Hospital Medical Information Network (UMIN) in Japan." Comment: Central allocation is explicitly described, which is a mechanism that prevents the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the study, neither the patient nor staff can be blinded." Comment: This was an open-label trial with no blinding of participants or personnel. The main outcomes included patient satisfaction and attitudes toward medication, which could plausibly be influenced by awareness of the intervention assignment, so performance bias is likely.

Quote: "Due to the nature of the study, neither the patient nor staff can be blinded." and "The primary outcome is the patient’s satisfaction at discharge as assessed by the Japanese version of the Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ-8J), a self-report scale"; also "The Japanese version of the DAI-10... is used to measure attitudes toward medication" and "the ward doctor undertakes routinely" for BPRS. Comment: The outcome assessor for the main patient-reported outcomes is the patient themselves, who was not blinded, so knowledge of allocation could directly influence reporting of satisfaction and medication attitudes. The clinician-rated BPRS was also not blinded. Although treatment continuation from records is objective, the key outcomes include subjective measures assessed without blinding, so detection bias is high.

Quote: "The data will be analysed on an intention-to-treat principle. Multiple imputation methods [28] will be used for imputing any missing information regarding outcome measures." Comment: The protocol explicitly plans ITT analysis with multiple imputation for missing outcome data, which is an appropriate method to handle attrition. The study also anticipated only 10% dropout in the sample size calculation, suggesting attrition was expected to be limited rather than large or obviously biasing.
